name: analyze
context: Verify unit files, parse timer expressions, inspect load paths, and debug manager behavior with systemd-analyze
info: This skill covers `systemd-analyze`, the diagnostic and validation surface for systemd managers and unit files. The most important verbs for autonomous agents are `verify` to catch unit file errors before activation, `calendar`, `timestamp`, and `timespan` to validate scheduling syntax, `unit-paths` to understand where units and drop-ins can load from, and `security` to score an installed unit's hardening posture. It also covers `blame`, `critical-chain`, `dump`, and `dot` for boot and dependency debugging. Agents use this skill before `daemon-reload`, when timer expressions are ambiguous, and when services or the boot path behave unexpectedly.
triggers: show dependency chain for a unit
body:
# systemd-analyze

`systemd-analyze` is the safest place to validate unit files and timer syntax before activation. It also provides a second layer of observability when `systemctl` and logs tell you something is wrong but not why the manager behaved that way.

```bash
systemd-analyze verify /etc/systemd/system/my-app.service
systemd-analyze calendar 'Mon..Fri 03:00'
systemd-analyze security my-app.service
systemd-analyze unit-paths
systemd-analyze critical-chain my-app.service
```

For topology or performance work, it also exposes `blame`, `dump`, `dot`, and `plot`. The key operational pattern is simple: verify first, reload second, start third. That prevents a broken unit file from becoming a failed runtime event when it could have been caught offline.

If a file validates but the unit still fails at runtime, continue with [[systemd/systemctl]] and [[systemd/journalctl]].

sections.title: Verify unit files
sections.body: Use `systemd-analyze verify FILE...` on unit files before `systemctl daemon-reload`. This catches syntax problems, missing references, and many structural mistakes early. It is the right first step after writing a new service or timer file or when a drop-in may have broken a previously working unit.

sections.title: Validate calendar and time syntax
sections.body: `systemd-analyze calendar`, `timestamp`, and `timespan` normalize the exact time syntax consumed by timer units. Use them before installing a timer when a schedule is expressed in natural language, when you want to preview the next elapse times, or when a string like `5h 30min` must be checked for correctness.

sections.title: Review service hardening
sections.body: `systemd-analyze security UNIT` scores the unit's sandboxing and isolation settings and highlights missing controls such as capability drops, filesystem restrictions, or service identity limits. Treat it as a fast feedback loop for hardening, not as a complete security proof.

sections.title: Boot and dependency debugging
sections.body: Use `blame` to see which units spent the most time activating, `critical-chain` to trace the time-critical dependency path, `dot` for graph output, and `dump` for a long human-readable snapshot of manager state. These commands are useful when a service waits on another unit, when boot is slower than expected, or when ordering relationships are unclear.

links.context: Verify services before daemon-reload and activation
Use unit-paths alongside load-path rules and override precedence
Move from offline verification into manager reload and activation
Use analyze verbs inside a broader debugging runbook

related.note: Service authoring should be followed by `systemd-analyze verify`
Timer expressions are easiest to validate with `calendar` and `timespan`
Troubleshooting patterns use analyze verbs to confirm structural causes

scripts.name: Analyze systemd
scripts.description: Run the most useful `systemd-analyze` verbs for validation and debugging

SYSTEMD_SCOPE.description: Manager scope: system, user, or global

SYSTEMD_ANALYZE_ACTION.description: Action such as verify, calendar, security, unit-paths, blame, or critical-chain

SYSTEMD_ANALYZE_UNITS.description: Newline-separated unit names for security or critical-chain

SYSTEMD_ANALYZE_PATTERNS.description: Newline-separated patterns for dump or dot

depends.reason: The wrapper is a bash script that invokes systemd-analyze
Understanding load paths and overrides improves interpretation of verify and unit-paths output

author: dojo-community
